---
title: "AI Literacy for Economists"
subtitle: "Modular lessons you can use, adapt, or drop into any course"
---

## What Is This?

A growing collection of self-contained modules for teaching economics students how to use AI effectively. Each module is designed to work on its own — pick what you need.

**For students**: Build real skills, not just familiarity. Understand what AI does, when it helps, and how to use it without losing the learning.

**For instructors**: Each module is a standalone lesson (~30–75 min) you can adopt as-is or adapt for your course. No sequence required.

## Modules

### Foundations (no coding required)

| Module | Description | Time |
|--------|-------------|------|
| [A1: What LLMs Actually Do](modules/a1-what-llms-do.qmd) | Tokens, prediction, and why AI hallucinates — mental models for economists | ~50 min |
| [A2: Prompting as Problem Specification](modules/a2-prompting.qmd) | Clear prompts = clear thinking. The prompt is your identification strategy | ~50 min |
| [A3: When AI Helps vs. Hurts Your Learning](modules/a3-when-to-use-ai.qmd) | Metacognition for the AI era — when to use it, when to struggle | ~30 min |

### Technical Foundations

| Module | Description | Time |
|--------|-------------|------|
| [B1: Terminal Basics](modules/b1-terminal-basics.qmd) | What is a shell, navigating files, basic commands, and why reproducibility matters | ~75 min |
| [B2: Git & GitHub Essentials](modules/b2-git-github.qmd) | Version control for economists — clone, commit, push, and why you should care | ~75 min |

### AI for Economics Workflows

| Module | Description | Time |
|--------|-------------|------|
| [C1: Literature Review & Synthesis](modules/c1-lit-review.qmd) | Using AI to survey a field — and catching what it gets wrong | ~50 min |
| [C2: Code Assistance (Stata/R)](modules/c2-code-assistance.qmd) | Debugging, translation, documentation — and when AI code is dangerous | ~50 min |
| [C3: Data Exploration & Cleaning](modules/c3-data-exploration.qmd) | Describing datasets, flagging anomalies — and why cleaning decisions are yours | ~50 min |

## Design Philosophy

1. **Each module stands alone.** No prerequisites, no required sequence.
2. **Economics-native examples.** Not "write a poem" — "debug this regression," "summarize this literature."
3. **Honest about limitations.** We teach when AI fails, not just when it shines.
4. **Platform-flexible.** Concepts transfer across tools. UVM-specific notes where relevant.

## For Instructors

Every module includes:

- **Learning objectives** aligned to AI literacy competencies
- **Suggested timing** and natural break points
- **Discussion questions** and reflection prompts
- **Adaptation notes** for different course contexts

Licensed [CC-BY 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/) — use freely with attribution.

## About

Created by [Emily Beam](https://eabeam.github.io), Department of Economics, University of Vermont.

Built with [Quarto](https://quarto.org). Source on [GitHub](https://github.com/eabeam/teaching-ai).
